titlestmt.titleproper: Guide to the Carroll and Helen Yerkes Papers
titlestmt.author: Compiled by Shari Laist

controlnote.p: This encoded findingaid is compliant with the Yale EAD Best Practice Guidelines, Version 1.0.

conventiondeclaration.abbr: finding aid prepared according to local divinity library descriptive practices.

descriptivenote.p: Finding aid description and metadata in Archives at Yale are licensed under a CC0 1.0 Universal (CC0 1.0) Public Domain Dedication. This means that anyone may copy, modify, and distribute this descriptive metadata without restriction or permission. The CC0 license is explained in full on the Creative Commons website: CC0 1.0 Universal (CC0 1.0) Public Domain Dedication. More information is available on our Help page.

maintenanceevent.eventdescription: This finding aid was produced using ArchivesSpace on Thursday November 12, 2020 at 23:04

did.abstract: Letters and collected material document the work of the Presbyterian Church in Shandong (Shantung) Province, China from 1869 to the 1940s. Carroll and Helen Yerkes were American Presbyterian missionaries in Shandong (Shantung) Province from 1904 to 1925. The parents of Helen Nevius Eckard Yerkes were Presbyterian missionaries stationed in Chefoo from 1869 to 1874.

accessrestrict.head: Conditions Governing Access
accessrestrict.p: The materials are open for research.

prefercite.head: Preferred Citation
prefercite.p: Carroll and Helen Yerkes Papers (RG 153), Special Collections, Yale Divinity School Library.

chronitemset.event: Born in Fox Chase, Philadelphia, PA
Attended International Conventions of Christian Endeavor at which he heard Dwight L. Moody, Robert E. Speer, Amos R. Wells and others who influenced his life.
Worked with settlers on the prairie of No. Dakota
Arrived in Shanghai as missionary for the Presbyterian Board of Foreign Missions
Opened station at Yixian, Shandong (Yihsien, Shantung Province)
Returned to U.S. because of Helen Yerkes' health
Served as pastor of various churches in New Jersey and Pennsylvania

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The Yerkes papers document the work of the Presbyterian Church in the Shandong (Shantung) Province of China from 1869-1874 when Helen Yerkes' parents were stationed in Chefoo and from 1904-1950. Although they left China in 1925, Yerkes stayed in contact with his missionary and Chinese friends. Yerkes was involved with evangelical work as well as the administrative tasks of running the station. Among his duties, he served on the China Council and acted as treasurer of the station. From 1923 until his departure from China, he worked with the American Consulate, R.P. Tenney, to secure the release of foreign captives after the wreck of the "Blue Express" on May 6, 1923 and continued to supply information about the bandit situation in his area.

processinfo.head: Processing Information
processinfo.p: Place names were modernized in the description, with the name originally used in the collection material or in an older version of the finding aid in parenthesis: e.g. "Beijing (Peking)" or "Benin (Dahomey)".

did.unittitle: Letters from Helen Yerkes' father, Leighton W. Eckard, to his family, from Chefoo
China Council Ad Interim Committee minutes
The China Council of the Presbyterian Church in the U.S.A.
Chinese documents - calling cards, correspondence, money, etc.
Chinese posters and prints, including "The Chinese Family", Mission tract "colored by one of my school boys", Biblical quotations, anatomical diagram, etc.
Print depicting "God of Agriculture and Silk Worm Culture", unidentified print
Station reports: Yixian (Yihsien) (including general China Station information, building diagrams, staff resumes)
Commission, marriage certificate, passports, Chinese documents, etc.
Material re: Yerkes' role in the release of captives from the wreck of the "Blue Express" on May 6, 1923; including information re: bandit situation around Yixian (Yihsien)
"Recollections of a Long Life" (autobiographical) and other biographical material and tributes
Sunday School class of Leighton Eckard
Scenes from Ichoufu, Yixian (Yihsien), Shanghai; coal mining, railroads, agriculture, markets, etc.
Student groups and hospital scenes, local church members (Yixian (Yihsien) and outstations)
